title: Setup Instructions: AI for Urban Health Course Website
text:
Step-by-step instructions for building, previewing, and deploying the course website.



Quarto is the tool that converts the .qmd files into a website.
Option A: Homebrew (macOS)
brew install quarto
Option B: Download installer
Go to https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/ and download the installer for your operating system.
Verify installation:
quarto --version
You should see a version number (1.4 or later recommended).





Go to github.com and sign in (or create an account)
Click the + button in the top right and select New repository
Name it something like ai-urban-health-course
Set it to Public (required for free GitHub Pages hosting)
Do NOT initialize with a README (we already have files)
Click Create repository





# Navigate to where you want the project
cd ~/Documents

# Clone the empty repo
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/ai-urban-health-course.git

# Enter the directory
cd ai-urban-health-course




Copy all the files from the course-website/ folder into your cloned repository:
# Copy all course files into the repo
cp -r "/Users/daq26/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-DrexelUniversity/Anthropic/AI for UH Course/course-website/"* .
Your repository should now contain:
ai-urban-health-course/
├── _quarto.yml
├── styles.css
├── index.qmd
├── day1.qmd
├── day2.qmd
├── day3.qmd
├── day4.qmd
├── day5.qmd
├── readings.qmd
├── resources.qmd
└── setup-instructions.md




Run the following command from inside the repository directory:
quarto preview
This will: - Render the website - Open it in your browser at http://localhost:#### - Watch for changes and auto-refresh
Press Ctrl+C in the terminal to stop the preview.




When you are ready to build the final version:
quarto render
This creates a docs/ folder containing the static HTML website. The docs/ folder is what GitHub Pages will serve.







Commit and push all files:

git add .
git commit -m "Initial course website"
git push origin main

Go to your repository on GitHub
Click Settings (gear icon)
In the left sidebar, click Pages
Under Source, select:

Branch: main
Folder: /docs

Click Save

Your site will be live at https://YOUR-USERNAME.github.io/ai-urban-health-course/ within a few minutes.





The typical workflow for making changes:
# 1. Edit the .qmd file(s) in your text editor or VS Code

# 2. Preview your changes
quarto preview

# 3. When satisfied, render the final version
quarto render

# 4. Commit and push
git add .
git commit -m "Updated Day 3 with additional activity"
git push origin main
The website will update automatically within a few minutes of pushing.


If you have Claude Code installed, you can make updates quickly:
# Example: Add a new announcement to the home page
claude "Add a callout box at the top of index.qmd announcing that Day 2 slides are now available for download"

# Example: Fix a broken link
claude "Check all links in resources.qmd and flag any that look incorrect"

# Example: Add a reading
claude "Add this reading to the Day 3 section of readings.qmd: Smith et al. 2024, 'AI Agents in Public Health', Journal of Public Health Informatics"

# After Claude Code makes changes:
quarto render
git add .
git commit -m "Updates via Claude Code"
git push origin main







Quarto is not in your PATH. Try:
# Check if it was installed
ls /usr/local/bin/quarto
ls /opt/homebrew/bin/quarto

# Or reinstall
brew reinstall quarto



If quarto render fails:

Check the error message carefully — it usually points to the exact file and line
Common issues: unclosed code blocks (missing ```), malformed YAML front matter, broken callout syntax
Try rendering a single file to isolate the problem: quarto render day1.qmd





Make sure the docs/ folder was committed and pushed
Check Settings > Pages to confirm the source is set correctly
It can take 2-5 minutes for changes to appear
Check the Actions tab in your repository for build status





Make sure styles.css is in the same directory as _quarto.yml
Check that _quarto.yml references it correctly: css: styles.css
Try a hard refresh in your browser (Cmd+Shift+R)




If you add images later:

Create an images/ folder in the repository
Place images there
Reference them in .qmd files as: ![Alt text](images/filename.png)
Make sure to git add images/ before committing






If you want to use a custom domain (e.g., ai-urbanhealth.drexel.edu):

In your repository, go to Settings > Pages
Under Custom domain, enter your domain
Work with Drexel IT to create a CNAME record pointing to YOUR-USERNAME.github.io
Check Enforce HTTPS








Task
Command




Preview site locally
quarto preview


Render site
quarto render


Commit changes
git add . && git commit -m "message"


Push to GitHub
git push origin main


Check Quarto version
quarto --version


Render single file
quarto render day1.qmd

title: Readings
text:
Readings are organized by day, with Required (current, short, do-before-class) and Recommended (deeper or historical) tiers.

Links go to the source where it is freely available online.
[Find it] links run a title search (use them if a direct link is paywalled or moves).
[Provided] items are distributed through the course materials — these include copyrighted articles and book excerpts we don’t post publicly.


No textbook purchase is required. Co-Intelligence is recommended, not required; its core ideas are captured in Mollick’s free essays and newsletter (below).





Mollick, E. (2025). I, Cyborg: Using Co-Intelligence. One Useful Thing. Read free

The free, current stand-in for the book: the jagged frontier, “centaurs vs. cyborgs,” and keeping a human in the loop.

World Health Organization. (2024). Ethics and governance of AI for health: Guidance on large multi-modal models. WHO. Available online

The updated, generative-AI-era successor to WHO’s 2021 guidance; read the executive summary.

Recommended: Topol, E. (2019). High-performance medicine. Nature Medicine, 25(1), 44–56. Available online · “How People Are Really Using AI in 2026.” [Provided]








Anthropic. (2024). Prompt Engineering Guide. Available online

Focus on “Be clear and direct” and “Use examples.”

Anthropic. (2025). Effective context engineering for AI agents. Available online

Why the course now centers context engineering over single prompts.






Mollick, E. (2024). Co-Intelligence, Ch. 1–2. Portfolio/Penguin. [Provided]
Vaswani, A., et al. (2017). Attention is all you need. NeurIPS 30. Available online — historical context only.
Governor Shapiro. (2024). Executive Order on AI governance for the Commonwealth. Available online
MIT Technology Review. (2026). 10 Breakthrough Technologies / What matters in AI right now. Find it — a quick snapshot of how fast things changed.









Jowsey, T., et al. (2025). We reject the use of generative AI for reflexive qualitative research. Available online

The current debate on AI in qualitative analysis; pairs with Activity 4.

Boykis, V. (2026). Running local models is good now. Read free (Find it) — open/local models for sensitive data.





OpenDataPhilly. (2024). Heat Vulnerability Index — Metadata & Documentation. Available online — review before Activity 3.
Gichoya, J. W., et al. (2022). AI recognition of patient race in medical imaging. Lancet Digital Health, 4(6). Available online
“Exploring Agent-Assisted Qualitative Analysis.” [Provided] — the constructive counterpoint to Jowsey et al.
Anthropic. (2025). Coding agents for data analysis. [Provided] / Find it
Meskó, B., & Topol, E. (2023). Regulatory oversight of LLMs in healthcare. npj Digital Medicine, 6. Available online — now largely superseded by WHO (2024).
Mollick, E. (2024). Co-Intelligence, Ch. 3. [Provided]









Anthropic. (2025). Claude Code Documentation. Available online — skim getting-started + capabilities.
Ji, Y. (“Peak”). (2025). Context Engineering for AI Agents: Lessons from Building Manus. Read free

What building a real agent actually takes.

Google. (2025). Google AI Studio Documentation. Available online — review before Activity 4.

title: Day 5: Responsible AI & Future Directions
text:
Ethics, bias, privacy, accountability, and your personal AI integration plan


1:00–4:00 PM EST
📊 Open the Day 5 slides ↗

title: Day 5: Responsible AI & Future Directions
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of today, you will be able to:

Describe the FAIR principles for data and how they apply to AI in public health
Explain how model cards and datasheets for datasets support transparency
Identify sources of bias in AI systems and their potential impact on health equity
Assess privacy risks when using AI with public health data, including HIPAA and GDPR considerations
Evaluate the trade-offs between AI capability and interpretability (the “black box” problem)
Discuss accountability frameworks for AI-assisted decisions in public health
Develop a personal plan for responsible AI integration in your work
Make AI-assisted work reproducible and transparent, including disclosure of AI use

title: Day 5: Responsible AI & Future Directions
section: Lecture: Responsible AI for Public Health
text:
Lecture: Responsible AI for Public Health

FAIR Data Principles and AI
The FAIR principles—Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable—originally developed for research data management, have direct relevance for AI in public health:

Findable: AI models and training data should be documented and discoverable
Accessible: Clear terms of use and access protocols
Interoperable: Models should work with standard data formats and tools
Reusable: Models and their outputs should be usable for new purposes with clear licensing


Model Cards and Datasheets
Two tools for AI transparency:

Model Cards (Mitchell et al., 2019): Standardized documentation for AI models that includes intended use, limitations, performance across demographic groups, and ethical considerations
Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru et al., 2021): Documentation for training datasets that describes how data was collected, from whom, for what purpose, and with what limitations







NoteWhy This Matters for Public Health



If you are using an AI model to inform a health decision—whether it is triaging patient complaints, prioritizing neighborhood interventions, or drafting public communications—you should be able to answer: What data was this model trained on? Does it perform equally well for all populations we serve? What are its known failure modes?





Sources of Bias in AI Systems
Bias can enter AI systems at every stage:

Historical bias: Training data reflects past inequities (e.g., health records from a system that underserved Black patients)
Representation bias: Some populations are overrepresented or underrepresented in training data
Measurement bias: Proxies used for outcomes may not mean the same thing across groups (e.g., healthcare costs as a proxy for health need)
Aggregation bias: Models that work well on average may fail for specific subgroups
Deployment bias: Models used in contexts different from what they were designed for
Feedback loops: Biased outputs influence future data collection, reinforcing the bias



Privacy: HIPAA, GDPR, and LLM-Specific Risks
Key privacy considerations when using AI in public health:

HIPAA: Protected Health Information (PHI) should never be entered into consumer AI tools without a Business Associate Agreement (BAA). Most free-tier AI tools explicitly state they may use your inputs for training.
GDPR: European data protection standards apply if you work with data from EU residents.
LLM-specific risks:

Training data inclusion: Your prompts may be used to train future models (check each platform’s data usage policy)
Memorization: LLMs can sometimes reproduce training data, including potentially sensitive information
Inference attacks: Seemingly anonymous prompts can reveal sensitive information in aggregate
Prompt injection: Malicious content can manipulate AI behavior




The Black Box Problem
Many AI systems—especially deep learning models—are difficult or impossible to fully explain. This creates challenges for:

Clinical decision support: Can you trust a recommendation you cannot explain?
Policy decisions: How do you justify an AI-informed policy to the public?
Legal accountability: Who is responsible when an opaque AI system makes an error?
Public trust: Will communities trust AI-informed health interventions?



Accountability
When AI assists with public health decisions, clear accountability is essential:

The human who acts on AI output bears responsibility for the consequences
Organizations need AI governance frameworks that define who can use AI, for what purposes, with what oversight
Documentation of AI use in decision-making processes should become standard practice
Audit mechanisms should be built in to review AI-assisted decisions

This maps onto the three inverse laws introduced on Day 1: non-anthropomorphism (the model has no judgment to defer to), non-deference (verify before acting), and non-abdication (you stay answerable for the result). Framing adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).


Environmental Costs
AI has significant environmental costs that are often overlooked:

Training large language models requires enormous computational resources
A single ChatGPT query uses significantly more energy than a Google search
Data centers require cooling and consume large amounts of water
These costs are disproportionately borne by communities near data centers



Community Co-Design
Responsible AI in public health requires engaging the communities being studied or served:

Nothing about us without us: Communities should be involved in designing AI systems that affect them
Participatory design: Include community members in defining problems, selecting data, and evaluating outputs
Transparency: Be clear with communities about when and how AI is being used
Benefit sharing: Ensure that the benefits of AI-assisted public health reach the communities that contributed their data

title: Day 5: Responsible AI & Future Directions
text:
Activity 1: Ethical Dilemma Scenarios

Activity 1: Ethical Dilemma Scenarios

Duration: ~45 minutes | Format: Small group discussion (15 min per dilemma, rotate through 2–3)

Goal: Grapple with realistic ethical challenges in AI-assisted public health.
Your group will discuss 2–3 of the following scenarios. For each:

Identify the core ethical tensions
Discuss what you would do and why
Consider what policies or guardrails could prevent the problem
Note areas of disagreement within your group



Dilemma 1: Hospital AI Chatbot Error
A Philadelphia hospital deploys an AI chatbot on its patient-facing website to help patients assess symptoms and determine whether they need emergency care. The chatbot correctly handles most cases, but tells a patient experiencing atypical heart attack symptoms (which are more common in women) to “rest and take over-the-counter pain medication.” The patient delays seeking care and suffers significant cardiac damage.
Discussion Questions:

Who is responsible—the hospital, the chatbot vendor, the physician who approved the system?
Should AI chatbots be used for symptom assessment at all? Under what conditions?
How should the system have handled uncertainty?
What testing should have been done before deployment?



Dilemma 2: Public Health Dashboard Failure
Your health department builds an AI-powered dashboard that predicts COVID-19 outbreaks by neighborhood, integrating wastewater surveillance, emergency department data, and social media activity. The model works well in wealthy neighborhoods with extensive data infrastructure but performs poorly in low-income communities with limited data sources, effectively creating a two-tiered surveillance system. Resources are allocated based on the dashboard’s predictions.
Discussion Questions:

Is it better to deploy an imperfect system that helps some neighborhoods, or wait for one that works equally well everywhere?
How does this scenario reflect broader patterns in health equity?
What data sources could improve performance in under-monitored neighborhoods?
How should you communicate the system’s limitations to decision-makers?



Dilemma 3: Mobile Location Data for Outbreak Tracking
During a syphilis outbreak concentrated in specific Philadelphia neighborhoods, an analyst proposes purchasing anonymized mobile phone location data to identify social networks and gathering places that might be linked to transmission. The data could help target testing and outreach. However, the neighborhoods most affected are predominantly communities of color with historical reasons to distrust public health surveillance.
Discussion Questions:

Does the public health benefit justify the surveillance implications?
What is the difference between “anonymized” and truly anonymous data?
How does the historical context (Tuskegee, other abuses) shape the ethical calculus?
What community engagement would be needed before proceeding?
What alternative approaches might achieve similar goals with less risk?



Dilemma 4: Optum Healthcare Algorithm Bias (Real Case)
A widely reported study (Obermeyer et al., 2019, Science) found that an algorithm used by Optum and many US health systems to identify patients who would benefit from “high-risk care management” programs was systematically biased against Black patients. The algorithm used healthcare costs as a proxy for healthcare needs. Because Black patients had historically received less healthcare spending (due to access barriers and systemic racism), the algorithm concluded they were healthier than equally sick White patients. At a given risk score, Black patients were significantly sicker than White patients.
Discussion Questions:

Why was using healthcare costs as a proxy for need problematic? What alternatives might work better?
How could this bias have been detected before deployment?
What does this case teach us about using historical data to train AI systems?
How should organizations audit AI systems for bias?

Prompt Set 2: Community Scenes

“A healthy family eating dinner”

“A low-income neighborhood”
“A thriving community”


Analyze the results:

For each generated image, note:











Prompt
Race/Ethnicity
Gender
Age
Setting
Stereotypes Present?














Discussion:


What patterns do you notice in how the AI depicts different roles, communities, and scenarios?
How do these patterns reflect (or amplify) societal biases?
What would be the impact of using biased AI-generated images in public health materials?
How can you mitigate this in practice?







ImportantKey Takeaway



AI image generation reflects the biases present in its training data, which is largely sourced from the internet. These biases can reinforce stereotypes about who belongs in professional roles, what communities “look like,” and whose health matters. Critical evaluation of AI outputs is essential.

section: Activity 5: Make Your Work Reproducible (with AI)
text:
Activity 5: Make Your Work Reproducible (with AI)

Activity 5: Make Your Work Reproducible (with AI)

Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Individual work + pair review

Goal: Take a piece of your own AI-assisted analysis or writing and make it transparent and reproducible — so a colleague (or future you) can rerun it and see exactly where AI was used.
Why it matters: Responsible AI use is not just about bias and privacy — it is also about rigor. If you cannot reproduce a result or say how it was produced, you cannot stand behind it. AI makes it faster to generate work and easier to lose track of how that work was made.

Steps

Pick one artifact — a recent analysis script, a report, or something you built this week.
Capture the environment. Record the software and package versions so it can be rebuilt:

Python: uv lock / uv export (or pip freeze > requirements.txt)
R: sessionInfo() output, or renv::snapshot()

Make it literate. Put code and narrative in one document that runs top to bottom (Quarto, R Markdown, or a Jupyter notebook) — no “run these cells out of order” surprises.
Log the AI use. Note where AI contributed (drafting, code, analysis) and write a short AI-use disclosure statement you could include in a paper or report.
Document data provenance. A short README: data source, access date, and which files are raw vs. derived.
Stress-test it. Use AI as an adversarial reader of your own work:

Review this analysis. List every external dependency, file path, hard-coded value, and manual step someone else would need to reproduce it. Flag anything that is not documented.

Then fix the gaps it finds.



Pair Review
Swap with a partner and try to follow each other’s README and reproducibility notes. Could you rerun it from scratch? What is missing?
Deliverable: A runnable literate document, an environment/lock file, a short README, and an AI-use disclosure statement.






NoteReflection



What would break if someone ran your project a year from now? Where did AI genuinely help reproducibility, and where could it create a false sense of completeness? For a deeper version, see Bonus Exercise 4: Reproducing a Published Analysis.

title: Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building
text:
Lecture: Agents, Context, and Tools

Chatbots vs. Agents
The AI landscape is rapidly moving from chatbots (reactive, single-turn, limited to text) to agents (proactive, multi-step, tool-using):








Feature
Chatbot
Agent




Interaction
Single turn Q&A
Multi-step task completion


Tools
None (text only)
Can use tools (search, code, APIs)


Memory
Limited to conversation
Can maintain context across tasks


Autonomy
Responds only when asked
Can take initiative, plan, and execute


Error handling
Stops at confusion
Can retry, adapt, self-correct



Why this matters for public health: Agents can do things like “pull the latest flu surveillance data, compare it to last year, draft a summary report, and email it to the team”—tasks that involve multiple steps, tools, and decision points.


Context Engineering: The 5 Components
Context engineering is the practice of designing the information environment that an AI system operates within. It is more fundamental than prompt engineering. The five key components:

System prompt / Instructions: What the AI should do, its role, its constraints
User input: The specific request or query
Retrieved knowledge: Relevant documents, data, or information pulled in for the task (RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
Tool definitions: What tools the AI can use and how to use them
Conversation history / Memory: What has happened before in the interaction







TipThink of It Like Briefing a New Employee



Context engineering is like preparing a briefing packet for a highly skilled new employee on their first day. You would not just say “do your job.” You would provide: their role description (system prompt), the specific task (user input), relevant background materials (retrieved knowledge), access to the tools and systems they need (tool definitions), and notes from previous meetings (conversation history).




Model Context Protocol (MCP)
MCP is an emerging open standard (created by Anthropic) that allows AI models to connect with external tools and data sources through a standardized interface. Think of it as a “USB port for AI”—a universal way to plug in capabilities.
Examples of MCP tools:

File system access (read/write documents)
Database queries
Web search
Calendar and email integration
Code execution
API calls to external services



Agent Skills
A Skill is a reusable folder of expertise you hand the agent. At minimum it is a SKILL.md file — a Markdown document with YAML frontmatter (a name and a description) and instructions in the body — plus optional scripts/ (code the agent can run) and references/ (documents loaded only when needed). When a task matches the description, the agent loads the skill on demand and follows it.
If MCP is how an agent reaches tools, a Skill is how it knows your way of doing a task. Where a prompt is a one-off instruction, a Skill is durable and reusable: write your de-identification checklist, your report format, or your STROBE/CONSORT reporting steps once, and the agent applies them every time — across chats, Claude Code, and Cowork.
You can write a SKILL.md by hand, or use the Skill Creator (a “skill that builds skills”) — it’s pre-installed in Claude Desktop and Cowork, and installable in Claude Code (/plugin install skill-creator). On claude.ai, upload a zipped skill folder under Settings → Customize → Skills.
Public-health skill ideas: a “de-identify before sharing” checklist, a plain-language health-communication rewriter, a grant Specific-Aims formatter, or a data-dictionary generator.


Agentic Harnesses: Cowork, Claude Code, Codex, and More
The same core idea — a model plus tools running in a loop — ships in several “harnesses” that differ mainly in where you work and how much autonomy they take. (Recall the three ways to work with AI from Day 1.)









Harness
What it is
Best for
Notes




Claude Code (Anthropic)
Terminal/CLI agent
Coding, data analysis, file automation in a repo
Reads CLAUDE.md; strong on coding benchmarks; Anthropic models


Cowork (Anthropic)
Desktop app agent
Non-coders: files, connectors, Skills, scheduled tasks
Runs each task as its own session; paid plan


Codex / Codex CLI (OpenAI)
Terminal agent
Long, “walk-away” autonomous runs (“goal mode”)
Strong Docker sandboxing; OpenAI models


Google Antigravity
Agent-first IDE + CLI/SDK
Orchestrating multiple agents; browser-testing what it builds
Free public preview; Gemini models (Gemini CLI folded into it)


OpenCoworker
Open-source desktop agent
Privacy-sensitive work; bring-your-own model
Free/self-hostable; use your API key or a local Ollama model



The differences that matter for choosing: surface (terminal vs. desktop vs. IDE), autonomy (one step vs. hours of unattended work), model lock-in (vendor-only vs. bring-your-own), and data control (cloud vs. fully local). For sensitive public-health data, weigh the last two heavily.


Scheduled Tasks
Agentic tools increasingly run on a schedule, not just on demand. In Cowork, you describe a task once, pick a cadence (hourly, daily, weekdays, weekly) or an event trigger (a new file, a webhook), and it runs automatically — each run with access to your files, connectors, and Skills. This turns a one-off (“summarize this week’s 311 complaints”) into standing automation (“every Monday, pull the new data, update the chart, and draft the email”). Schedule only work you’d be comfortable having run with a human reviewing the output — not irreversible actions.


Verification Is the Bottleneck
Agents generate far faster than you can check, so verification — not generation — becomes the limiting step and the real skill. Fluent output is not the same as correct output, and the higher the stakes, the higher your burden of proof. One of the most reliable uses of a model is to turn it on its own work: ask it to be an adversarial reader (“argue why this analysis is wrong”), to find the weakest claim, the missing control, or the unchecked assumption in your code, draft, or study design — then you decide.
Adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).

Activity 1: Context Engineering Exercise

Activity 1: Context Engineering Exercise

Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Small group design exercise

Goal: Design a context package for a specialized public health AI assistant.

Scenario
You are designing a “Neighborhood Health Assessment AI Assistant” for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. This agent should be able to:

Pull demographic and health data for any Philadelphia neighborhood
Compare indicators across neighborhoods
Generate assessment reports with visualizations
Identify health equity concerns
Recommend evidence-based interventions



Your Task
In your small group, design the five context components for this agent:
1. System Prompt / Instructions

What role should the agent assume?
What are its core capabilities?
What constraints should it have? (e.g., never provide individual-level data, always cite sources, flag uncertainty)

2. User Input Examples

Write 3 example queries a health department analyst might ask this agent

3. Retrieved Knowledge

What documents, datasets, or knowledge bases should this agent have access to?
How often should they be updated?

4. Tool Definitions

What tools should this agent be able to use? (e.g., database query, mapping, chart generation, report writing)

5. Conversation History / Memory

What should the agent remember across conversations?
What should it forget (for privacy/security)?



Share Out
Each group presents their context design in 2 minutes. Class discusses: What did groups prioritize differently? What components were hardest to define?

title: Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building
text:
Activity 2: Claude Code CLI Walkthrough

Activity 2: Claude Code CLI Walkthrough

Duration: ~25 minutes | Format: Instructor demonstration (follow along if you have Claude Code installed)

Goal: See how a command-line AI tool can perform data analysis, write code, and generate documents directly on your computer.

What is Claude Code?
Claude Code is a command-line interface (CLI) tool from Anthropic that lets you interact with Claude directly from your terminal. Unlike the web-based chat, Claude Code can:

Read and write files on your computer
Execute code and see results
Navigate your file system
Chain multiple operations together
Work with local data that never leaves your machine (though prompts are still sent to Anthropic’s servers)



Demo Outline
The instructor will demonstrate:
1. Data Analysis and Visualization
claude "Load the Philadelphia HVI dataset from data/philadelphia_hvi.csv.
       Summarize it and generate a bar chart of the 10 census
       tracts with the highest hvi_score. Save the chart
       as hvi_top10.png"
2. Document Generation
claude "Read the analysis results in output/ and draft a
       2-page executive summary for city council. Include
       the key findings, a table of the most vulnerable
       tracts, and three policy recommendations.
       Save as executive_summary.docx"
3. Multi-Step Workflow
claude "Download the latest flu surveillance data from CDC,
       compare Philadelphia's rates to the national average,
       create a trend visualization, and draft a weekly
       update email for the department director"


Key Takeaways

CLI tools are powerful for integrating AI into existing workflows
They work with your local files and tools
They enable automation of repetitive analysis-report-communicate cycles
They require some comfort with the command line (but AI can help with that too)







NoteInstructor Walkthrough: Running the Claude Code Demo Live





This is a step-by-step guide for the instructor to run the demo end-to-end on a real dataset. It uses a small sample Heat Vulnerability dataset so every command actually executes. Total live time: ~20–25 minutes. (Verified against Claude Code documentation, 2026; the CLI changes often, so test once the day before.)

Before class (15 minutes, once)

Install (needs Node.js v18+; check with node --version):
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
claude --version          # confirm it installed
Do not use sudo. If you hit permission errors, use nvm or reconfigure npm’s global prefix.
Authenticate. Run claude once; a browser opens to approve login. This works with a Claude Pro/Max or Team/Enterprise subscription (recommended for teaching), or with an API key from the Console.

Cost gotcha: if ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set in your shell, it overrides your subscription and you’ll be billed per-token. For a subscription demo, unset ANTHROPIC_API_KEY first.

Build the demo folder and drop in the data (download philadelphia_hvi.csv — the real OpenDataPhilly HVI: 384 Philadelphia tracts, 2023):
mkdir -p ~/cli-demo/data && cd ~/cli-demo
# move the downloaded file into ~/cli-demo/data/philadelphia_hvi.csv
Dry-run it once so there are no surprises live, then /clear before class.



Live demo (project on screen, narrate as you go)
Step 0 — Start in the demo folder (1 min). Open the terminal in ~/cli-demo and launch the REPL:
cd ~/cli-demo
claude
Point out the prompt is now waiting, and that Claude can see this folder. Type /help to show the slash commands, then /cost to show that usage is tracked.
Step 1 — Explore the data (read-only; ~4 min). Paste:

Look at data/philadelphia_hvi.csv. How many records are there, what is the unit of analysis, what columns are present, and are there any data-quality issues? Don’t write any files yet — just summarize.

What to narrate: Claude reads the file without asking permission (reads are allowed by default), but it has not changed anything. This is the safe way to start: explore before you act. Tie back to context engineering (Activity 1) — it only knows what’s in the folder plus your instructions.
Step 2 — Generate a chart (first permission prompt; ~6 min). Paste:

Create a horizontal bar chart of the 10 tracts with the highest hvi_score, labeled by name10 (the tract number). Use Python (matplotlib), save it as figures/hvi_top10.png, and create the figures/ folder if needed.

What to narrate: Claude now wants to write a script and run it, so it pauses and shows you the command and the file it will create. Approve it in front of the class — this is the human-in-the-loop checkpoint and the single most important thing to show. Open figures/hvi_top10.png afterward.
Step 3 — Draft a brief from the findings (~5 min). Paste:

Using what you found, draft a one-page plain-language brief for City Council on heat vulnerability, with the top 5 tracts (by name10) in a table and three policy recommendations. Save it as brief.md.

What to narrate: Same approval step for the new file. Then open brief.md and read a recommendation aloud, then push back: ask the class “is that number actually in our data?” Open the CSV and check. This is the verification-is-the-bottleneck point from earlier — fluent output still needs checking, especially before anything reaches a decision-maker.
Step 4 — Show the controls (~3 min).

/permissions — how to pre-approve or block specific commands.
Press Shift+Tab to cycle to plan mode (read-only): Claude proposes a plan but cannot edit. Good for “think before you touch my files.”
/clear to wipe context; /cost to show total session cost; /exit (or Ctrl+D) to quit.



Alternative: run it inside Positron (recommended for R/Python users)
Claude Code is still the CLI here — you just host it in Positron (Posit’s R/Python IDE) instead of a bare terminal. For a data-science audience this is usually the better venue: students stay in a familiar environment, and Claude’s edits and outputs appear right in the IDE.

Open the demo folder in Positron: File → Open Folder → ~/cli-demo.
Open the integrated terminal (Terminal → New Terminal, or Ctrl+`) and run claude there. Claude Code auto-detects the IDE (via a lock file in ~/.claude/ide/), so it knows which file you have open and shows its edits as diffs in the editor — which is much clearer to project than a terminal diff.
Keep it in your language. Add to your prompts: “Use R and ggplot2; save the script as a .R file and run it with Rscript” (or “use Python”). Run the same four steps as above.
Verify in the IDE. Open philadelphia_hvi.csv in Positron’s data viewer, let the chart land in the Plots pane, and run one line of R/Python yourself to confirm Claude’s numbers — a clean, visible version of the verification step.


Caveat: Claude runs commands in that terminal’s shell, so the R/Python it uses is whatever is on your PATH, not necessarily Positron’s selected interpreter. Confirm Rscript (or your Python environment) works in that terminal before class.

Positron also ships a built-in Positron Assistant (a native in-IDE chat/agent, bring-your-own Claude API key). That’s a different flavor from the agentic-CLI lesson, so keep Claude Code as the tool you demo — but it’s worth mentioning to students as another option.


If something breaks (fallbacks)

Can’t install / locked-down laptop: run the same prompts against claude.ai or Google AI Studio by uploading philadelphia_hvi.csv. You lose the local-file/automation angle but keep the analysis narrative.
No internet in the room: pre-record the run, or screenshot each step into the slides as a backup.
Want a no-prompt, scripted version: claude -p "summarize data/philadelphia_hvi.csv" runs once and exits — useful to show automation, but do the interactive version first so students see the approval prompts.
Don’t use --dangerously-skip-permissions in class except to name it and explain why skipping approvals is risky.



The three points to land

Agentic CLIs act on your real files and tools, not just chat text.
The permission prompt is the safety boundary — you stay in the loop on every write and command.
Generation is cheap; verification is the job. Always check the numbers before the output leaves the room.

text:
Activity 4: Google AI Studio Agent Building

Activity 4: Google AI Studio Agent Building

Duration: ~30 minutes | Format: Hands-on (using your free Google AI Studio account)

Goal: Build a simple AI agent with system instructions and structured behavior using Google AI Studio.

Instructions

Go to Google AI Studio
Click “Create New” and select a structured prompt or chat-based prompt



Option A: Disease Prediction Agent
Build an agent that helps public health analysts assess disease outbreak risk:
System Instructions:

You are an infectious disease risk assessment assistant for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Your role is to help analysts evaluate potential outbreak risks based on surveillance data and environmental factors.
When analyzing a potential outbreak: 1. Ask clarifying questions about the data (number of cases, location, time period, demographics affected) 2. Compare to historical baselines 3. Identify potential risk factors 4. Assess the likelihood of sustained transmission 5. Recommend specific response actions based on Philadelphia’s outbreak response protocols
Always cite the evidence basis for your assessments. Always flag uncertainty. Never provide definitive predictions—use probabilistic language.

Test your agent with scenarios like: > “We’ve seen 15 cases of Legionella in the Kensington area over the past two weeks. The previous baseline for this period is 2-4 cases. What should we be looking at?”


Option B: Environmental Health Risk Assessor
Build an agent focused on environmental health:
System Instructions:

You are an environmental health risk assessment assistant. You help public health analysts evaluate environmental exposures and their potential health impacts in Philadelphia neighborhoods.
For each assessment: 1. Identify the exposure pathway (air, water, soil, food) 2. Assess the exposed population (size, vulnerability factors) 3. Evaluate exposure duration and intensity 4. Review known health effects from the scientific literature 5. Recommend monitoring, mitigation, and communication strategies
Always specify the strength of evidence for health effects. Distinguish between established and emerging science.



Reflection

How does providing system instructions change the AI’s behavior?
What guardrails did you include? What would you add?
How could this be useful in your daily work?
What are the risks of relying on such an agent?

text:
Activity 5: Local AI with Ollama

Activity 5: Local AI with Ollama

Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Demonstration + optional hands-on installation

Goal: Understand why local AI models matter for sensitive data and see how to run them.

Why Local Models?
When you use ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini, your prompts and data are sent to external servers. For many public health tasks, this is fine. But for work involving:

Protected Health Information (PHI)
Personally identifiable data
Pre-publication research data
Sensitive agency communications
Data covered by IRB protocols

…you may need AI that runs entirely on your own machine, with no data leaving your computer.


What is Ollama?
Ollama is a free, open-source tool that lets you run AI models locally on your laptop. It supports many models, including:

Llama 3.x / Llama 4 (Meta) — good general-purpose models
Gemma 4 (Google) — lightweight and capable; runs well on a laptop
Qwen / DeepSeek — strong open models (note: developed in China; data stays local when run via Ollama)
Mistral — fast and efficient
Phi (Microsoft) — optimized for smaller hardware



Installation (for those who can install software)
# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install ollama

# Or download from https://ollama.com/download

# Start Ollama
ollama serve

# Pull and run a model (pick one that fits your laptop)
ollama run qwen3          # strong default; try qwen3:4b on 8 GB laptops
ollama run gemma4         # Google's efficient option
System Requirements:

At least 8 GB RAM (16 GB recommended)
5–10 GB free disk space per model
macOS, Linux, or Windows



Browser-Based Alternatives (for restricted computers)
If you cannot install software on your work computer:

Google AI Studio: Free, browser-based, runs Gemini models with reasonable privacy policies
HuggingChat: Free, open-source models, browser-based
Note: These still send data to external servers but may have stronger privacy protections than consumer products



Demo
The instructor will demonstrate:

Installing and running Ollama
Chatting with a local model
Comparing response quality to cloud-based models (trade-offs: speed, quality, privacy)
When local models are “good enough” vs. when you need cloud models







TipAdvanced companion: agentic workflows on a local model (notebook)



Want to go further than chatting? This Jupyter notebook builds real agentic workflows on a local Ollama model (a laptop-friendly model like Gemma 4 or Qwen3) — everything runs on your machine, free and private. You pick an everyday triage task — 311 complaints, food-safety violations, or pedestrian-injury surveillance — and work it through three patterns:

One structured-output prompt that triages one report against your rule (Pydantic + JSON mode).
A multi-agent crew (CrewAI) — a triage agent and a documentation agent collaborating.
A state machine (LangGraph) — the same workflow as an explicit graph with a conditional branch.

Plus a tool-calling agent (a keyword search + a triage counter over your queue) that previews what MCP standardizes, and a “try it yourself” exercise. It ties directly to the Day 3 concepts: the agent loop, context engineering, tools/MCP, guardrails, and verification.
Run it in Positron, VS Code, or Jupyter. Install once with pip install ollama crewai langgraph langchain-ollama pydantic. Best as an optional advanced track or a live instructor demo.








NoteFor IT-Restricted Computers



Many government and health department computers restrict software installation. If you cannot install Ollama on your work computer, the browser-based alternatives above provide a middle ground. For truly sensitive work, talk to your IT department about setting up a local AI environment on a secure server.

title: Day 1: Foundations of AI
text:
Lecture: AI Concepts and History

What Is Artificial Intelligence?
AI is a broad field encompassing any system that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence. We will walk through the key layers:

Artificial Intelligence (AI): The broadest category—machines that can sense, reason, act, and adapt
Machine Learning (ML): Systems that learn patterns from data rather than following explicit rules

Supervised learning: Learning from labeled examples (e.g., classifying chest X-rays)
Unsupervised learning: Finding patterns without labels (e.g., clustering neighborhoods by health outcomes)
Reinforcement learning: Learning through trial and reward (e.g., optimizing treatment protocols)

Deep Learning: ML using neural networks with many layers, enabling learning from raw data (images, text, audio)
Foundation Models / Large Language Models (LLMs): Models trained on massive datasets that can be adapted to many tasks



The Transformer Revolution
In 2017, the paper “Attention Is All You Need” introduced the transformer architecture, which enabled:

Processing entire sequences in parallel (not word by word)
The “attention mechanism”—allowing models to focus on relevant parts of the input
Scaling to billions of parameters, creating increasingly capable models

This architecture underlies ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and essentially all modern LLMs.


Why Urban Health Needs AI
Urban public health generates enormous amounts of data—surveillance records, community surveys, environmental monitoring, clinical data, policy documents—yet many departments are resource-constrained. AI can help with:

Rapid literature synthesis and evidence review
Data analysis and visualization with limited statistical staff
Drafting reports, communications, and policy briefs
Identifying patterns in large datasets
Translating materials for diverse communities
Streamlining routine administrative tasks







TipKey Concept: AI as a Tool, Not a Replacement



AI is most effective when it augments human expertise rather than replacing it. Throughout this course, we will focus on human-in-the-loop approaches where AI assists with tasks but humans make the final decisions.




A Working Vocabulary
A few terms you will hear all week: a model is the trained system itself (Claude, GPT, Gemini) — a pattern-completion engine, not a database; a token is a chunk of text (~¾ of a word) counted for usage and limits; a prompt is what you send, including any context; the context window is how much it can hold in mind at once; a session is one conversation (no memory across sessions unless you build it); and an agent is a model given tools and a goal that it pursues in steps (Day 3).


Three Ways to Work With AI
The same model behaves differently depending on where you meet it: in a chat window (browser; ask, paste, generate; can’t see your files), inside your tools (an editor or app that sees and edits your work in place), or agentically (you give a goal and it plans and runs multi-step work). The move from “ask and copy” to “delegate and verify” is the biggest shift this year.


The Three Inverse Laws of AI
Asimov’s laws constrain robots to protect people; these “inverse laws” constrain us: non-anthropomorphism (don’t attribute judgment or intent to the model), non-deference (never treat output as authoritative without verification), and non-abdication (you remain responsible for the result — “the AI told me so” is never a defense). We return to these on Day 5.
Vocabulary, the three modes, and the inverse laws are adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).


Philadelphia AI Policy Discussion
The Commonwealth of Pennsylvania has issued executive guidance on AI use in state agencies. We will review:

Current state-level AI governance frameworks
How Philadelphia city agencies are approaching AI adoption
Implications for your daily work and data handling

Prompt 3 (Creative):

title: Day 1: Foundations of AI
text:
Activity 2: Prompt Engineering Practice

Activity 2: Prompt Engineering Practice

Duration: ~40 minutes | Format: Guided practice with partner discussion

Goal: Learn and practice the PTCF framework for writing effective prompts.

The PTCF Framework
Good prompts have four components:








Component
Description
Example




Persona
Who should the AI act as?
“You are a public health epidemiologist…”


Task
What specifically should it do?
“Write a 500-word executive summary…”


Context
What background information is needed?
“This is for the city council health committee…”


Format
How should the output be structured?
“Use bullet points with headers, include a recommendation section…”





Practice Prompts
Try these prompts using the PTCF framework. Start with the “basic” version, then improve it:
Scenario 1: Herd Immunity Explanation
Basic prompt: “Explain herd immunity.”
Improved prompt (try writing your own first, then compare):

Persona: You are a community health educator working in a diverse urban neighborhood in Philadelphia.
Task: Explain the concept of herd immunity to a group of parents at a community health fair.
Context: Many parents in this community have questions about childhood vaccinations. Reading levels vary widely. Some community members have expressed vaccine hesitancy based on misinformation on social media.
Format: Write a 200-word explanation at an 8th-grade reading level. Use an analogy. End with one clear call to action. Avoid medical jargon.

Scenario 2: Vaccination Social Media Campaign
Basic prompt: “Write a social media post about flu shots.”
Your improved PTCF version: Write a prompt that specifies the audience (e.g., elderly residents in South Philadelphia), the platform (e.g., Facebook), the tone, and the desired call to action.
Scenario 3: Air Quality Executive Summary
Basic prompt: “Summarize air quality data.”
Your improved PTCF version: Specify who you are (e.g., environmental health analyst), what data you are summarizing (e.g., PM2.5 readings from 2024-2025 across Philadelphia neighborhoods), who the audience is (e.g., City Council), and what format the summary should take (e.g., 1-page brief with key findings, a table, and policy recommendations).


Advanced Techniques
After practicing the basics, try these:

Chain-of-thought prompting: Add “Think through this step by step” to complex analytical questions
Few-shot prompting: Give 1–2 examples of the output you want before asking for a new one
Structured output: Ask for JSON, CSV, or specific table formats
Iterative refinement: Start with a draft prompt, review the output, and revise the prompt







NoteReproducibility Note



AI outputs are not perfectly reproducible—the same prompt can generate different responses each time. For work that requires consistency, save your prompts, document which model and version you used, and note the date. Some platforms offer a “temperature” setting that affects randomness.

Activity 3: AI for Professional Writing

Activity 3: AI for Professional Writing

Duration: ~30 minutes | Format: Individual work + pair sharing

Goal: Practice using AI as a writing assistant for real public health work products.

Instructions
Choose one of the following tasks (or bring your own de-identified writing task):
Option A: Draft a Public Health Report Section
Ask AI to help you draft a section of a report on a topic relevant to your work. For example:

You are a public health analyst at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Draft the “Background” section (300–400 words) for a report on childhood asthma rates in Philadelphia. Include relevant national and local statistics, known risk factors in urban environments, and health equity considerations. Cite sources where possible and flag any statistics that should be verified.

Option B: Edit and Improve Existing Writing
Paste a paragraph from a real (de-identified) work document and ask the AI to:

Improve clarity and readability
Adjust the tone for a specific audience
Identify gaps in the argument
Suggest stronger transitions

Option C: Write a Policy Brief

Draft a 1-page policy brief recommending that Philadelphia expand its network of cooling centers based on heat vulnerability data. Include: an executive summary (2–3 sentences), the problem statement, key evidence, and 3 specific recommendations. The audience is City Council members who are not public health experts.



Pair Discussion
After completing your writing task, share with a partner:

What prompt did you use? How did you refine it?
What did the AI do well? What needed human editing?
Would you use this approach in your actual work? Why or why not?









WarningReminder: De-Identify Your Data



If you bring a real work document to this activity, make sure it contains no protected health information (PHI), personally identifiable information (PII), or confidential agency data. When in doubt, create a fictional scenario based on your real work.

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 1 Challenges


Exercise 1 — Cross-Platform AI Benchmark

Day 1 · Intermediate · ~45 min · Tools: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity

Goal: Compare how four AI platforms handle the same public-health task, and decide which to use for which kind of work.
Why it matters: You will inevitably choose among AI platforms for different tasks. A structured benchmark lets you decide from evidence rather than anecdote.
Steps

Build a scoring rubric. Rate each response 1–5 on six dimensions:

Accuracy (facts and statistics are verifiable), Completeness (covers all parts), Actionability (specific enough to act on), Appropriate uncertainty (acknowledges limits), Source quality (cites real, relevant sources), Public-health framing (uses sound epidemiological concepts).

Run the same prompt on all four (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity). Paste this exactly:

You are advising the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. A cluster of 47 cases of acute gastrointestinal illness has been reported in 72 hours across three ZIP codes in South Philadelphia. Preliminary interviews suggest a common food source. Design a rapid investigation plan covering: (1) immediate actions in the first 24 hours, (2) epidemiological investigation steps, (3) laboratory testing priorities, (4) communication strategy for the public and providers, and (5) criteria for escalating to state and federal authorities.

Record the exact prompt, date/time, and model version for each, and save each full response.
Score and analyze. Fill in the rubric and write a ~400–500 word comparison: which gave the most operationally useful plan; which handled uncertainty best; did any hallucinate statistics, cite non-existent guidelines, or make unsafe recommendations; and which you would recommend for which task.

Hints

Open all four in separate tabs and paste the identical prompt to avoid wording drift.
Watch specifically for invented citations and fabricated local statistics — the most common, most dangerous failure.

Done when

Rubric scored for all four platforms
Summary table comparing scores
~400–500 word comparison with a per-task recommendation

Deliverable: A document with your completed rubric, a summary table, and the comparison report.
Reflection: If you ran this next month, would the results hold? What does that imply about relying on platform comparisons? How would your rubric change for a clinical vs. an epidemiological audience?




Exercise 2 — Structured Output Engineering

Day 1 · Advanced · ~60 min · Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, a text editor

Goal: Build prompts that reliably turn messy public-health text into consistent, machine-readable JSON.
Why it matters: Public-health work constantly involves pulling structured facts out of unstructured text — case reports, surveillance summaries, meeting notes. Reliable structured output is a high-value, reusable skill.
Steps

Work from this report excerpt:

Between January and March 2026, the Philadelphia Department of Public Health received 23 reports of elevated blood lead levels (BLLs above 5 mcg/dL) in children under 6 in the Kensington neighborhood. Of these, 8 had BLLs of 5–9 mcg/dL, 12 of 10–19 mcg/dL, and 3 of 20 mcg/dL or above. Affected children ranged from 8 months to 5 years old. Home inspections found deteriorating lead paint in 18 homes, 14 built before 1950. The Department issued abatement orders for all confirmed properties and referred families to the lead poisoning prevention program.

Design a JSON schema that captures every key data point (counts, ranges, ages, housing facts, actions). Then write an extraction prompt, for example:

Extract the data from the report below into JSON matching exactly this schema: {schema}. Use null for anything not stated. Return only valid JSON, no commentary.

Test and refine until the output is valid, parseable JSON.
Build a 3-step chain: (1) extract raw text → JSON; (2) enrich — add CDC blood-lead action-level classifications, relevant Philadelphia housing rules, and national benchmarks; (3) generate — turn the enriched JSON into a formatted departmental briefing.
Stress test with a second, different report (ask the AI to generate one on another topic). Note which parts of your prompts generalize and which are topic-specific; revise for robustness.

Hints

Ask for “only valid JSON, no prose” — stray commentary is the usual reason parsing breaks.
Validate each step’s output is usable as the next step’s input before chaining further.

Done when

Schema produces valid JSON on the lead report
All three chain steps run end to end
Chain still works on a second, different report

Deliverable: Your schema, the full text of each prompt, the actual outputs at each step, and notes on errors and fixes.
Reflection: What was hardest for the AI to extract accurately? How would you validate AI extraction against the source text in a production workflow?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 2 Challenges


Exercise 3 — Multi-Source Data Integration with AI

Day 2 · Advanced · ~90 min · Tools: Claude or ChatGPT; R, Python, or Stata

Goal: Link environmental, health, and infrastructure datasets into one analysis with AI assistance.
Why it matters: Real public-health analysis rarely uses a single dataset. Joining across sources is essential for understanding health disparities — and the hard part is usually reconciling mismatched geographies.
Steps

Get the data. Use the provided Philadelphia HVI CSV (census-tract level), or download the real Heat Vulnerability by Census Tract plus 311 Service Requests (filter to environmental/housing, last 12 months) and one dataset of your choice (demographics, crashes, tree canopy) from OpenDataPhilly.
Examine each and document the geographic unit (tract, ZIP, point) and possible join keys.
Have the AI plan the join. Share the data dictionaries and ask:

These datasets use different geographic units ({list them}). Recommend the best strategy to join them, write code to harmonize the geographic identifiers, handle missing data, and add data-quality checks (record counts before/after the merge, key-variable distributions). Use {R / Python / Stata}.

Run it, and document every change you made to the AI’s code and why.
Analyze across datasets — e.g., correlation between HVI and 311 complaint density, or clusters where multiple risks converge. Produce at least two visuals (one map, one chart). Have the AI draft an interpretation, then check it for accuracy and appropriate causal language.

Hints

Converting between geographies (e.g., tract ↔︎ ZIP) loses information — ask the AI to state its crosswalk assumptions explicitly.
Always compare record counts before and after a merge; silent row loss is the classic join bug.

Done when

Datasets joined with documented keys
Two visualizations produced (one spatial)
Interpretation reviewed for causal-language errors

Deliverable: A reproducible document (R Markdown, Jupyter notebook, or Stata .do) with acquisition, merge code, visuals, and a short narrative.
Reflection: What was lost converting between geographic units? Where did the AI make incorrect assumptions? What dataset would strengthen this?




Exercise 4 — Reproducing a Published Epidemiologic Analysis

Day 2 · Expert · ~90 min · Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, Perplexity; R, Python, or Stata

Goal: Use AI to reproduce a published analysis from open data — and surface where it helps and where it introduces errors.
Why it matters: Reproducibility is core to rigor. AI can speed it up but also introduces subtle code errors you have to catch.
Steps

Pick a study that: was published in the last 5 years, uses public data (BRFSS, NHANES, CDC WONDER, Census/ACS), reports at least one regression, and is relevant to urban health. Record the citation, DOI, and data source.
Have the AI parse the methods. Paste the methods section and ask:

From this methods section, identify the exact dataset, variables, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and statistical models. Then write {R / Python / Stata} code to download the data and reproduce the main analysis.

Run and troubleshoot — expect to iterate. Document every change. Build a comparison table: variable/parameter, published result, your result, difference.
Write up (500–700 words): what reproduced, what differed and why, what methodological details were missing from the paper, and how much time AI saved.

Hints

Start with the single headline result, not the whole paper — get one number to match first.
Differences often come from undocumented data-cleaning choices; note them as findings, not failures.

Done when

Data downloaded and analysis runs
Results comparison table complete
Write-up explains matches and differences

Deliverable: Citation, annotated code, results comparison table, and the written report.
Reflection: What does this reveal about reproducibility in the field? Could AI-assisted reproduction become part of peer review?




Exercise 5 — Advanced Qualitative Coding Challenge

Day 2 · Advanced · ~60 min · Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, a spreadsheet

Goal: Develop a codebook, apply it, and measure how well AI coding agrees with your own.
Why it matters: AI-assisted qualitative coding raises real questions about reliability, validity, and whether AI captures the nuance an experienced researcher brings.
Steps

Get transcript text. Use the provided climate focus-group transcript, or these short excerpts on heat-safety barriers:

Speaker 1: “When it gets really hot, the elderly folks in my area just stay inside with the windows closed. They are afraid to use the AC because of the bill. One lady told me she’d rather sweat than get a $300 bill.”
Speaker 2: “Transportation is huge. Even if there’s a cooling center, how do you get there if you don’t drive? The bus routes in North Philly don’t go near them.”
Speaker 3: “Communication is a problem. Alerts go out in English, but half my clients speak Spanish or Vietnamese, and ‘excessive heat warning’ means nothing to them.”

Develop a codebook. Ask the AI for an inductive codebook, then review and finalize 8–12 codes with clear definitions.
Apply it two ways. Code additional excerpts (ask the AI to generate realistic continuations) with the AI, and code the same excerpts yourself independently. Put both in a spreadsheet coding matrix.
Measure agreement. Calculate Cohen’s kappa or percent agreement between you and the AI.
Write a 200–300 word methods paragraph describing the AI-assisted approach, reliability assessment, and limitations.

Hints

Lock the codebook before coding the comparison excerpts, or your kappa is meaningless.
Look hardest at where you and the AI disagree — that’s where the method’s limits show.

Done when

Finalized codebook (8–12 codes with definitions)
Two independently coded transcripts in a spreadsheet
Inter-rater reliability calculated
Methods paragraph written

Deliverable: Codebook, coded transcripts, reliability statistics, and methods paragraph.
Reflection: At what agreement level would you trust AI as a primary coder? What are the IRB implications of using AI on identifiable qualitative data?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 3 Challenges


Exercise 6 — Building an Automated Data Pipeline

Day 3 · Expert · ~90 min · Tools: Claude Code, Python, OpenDataPhilly API

Goal: Use an agentic CLI to build a working data pipeline that downloads, cleans, analyzes, and reports — on its own.
Why it matters: Departments repeat the same data chores constantly. Automating them frees analyst time for interpretation and action.
Steps

Scope the pipeline. It should: (a) acquire 311 service requests (last 90 days, environmental/housing) from the OpenDataPhilly API; (b) clean — handle missing values, standardize addresses, validate dates, drop duplicates, geocode to census tract; (c) analyze — complaint count and per-capita rate by tract, most common type, median response time, week-over-week trend; (d) generate an HTML report with a table, bar chart, time series, and narrative.
Have Claude Code build it. In a project folder, run claude and ask:

Build a Python pipeline that {paste the four steps above}. Use clear functions, add logging and error handling, and make the date range and complaint filter configurable. Show me the script before running anything.

Review before running. Check API endpoints, error handling, logging, and parameters. Then run and debug.
Add two improvements — e.g., retry logic, response caching, or extra validation checks.
Document (400–500 words): architecture, dependencies, error handling, maintenance, limitations.

Hints

Approve Claude Code’s file writes and commands one at a time the first run, so you see exactly what it does.
Cache the API response while developing so you are not re-downloading on every test run.

Done when

Pipeline runs end to end and produces the HTML report
At least two improvements added
Technical doc written

Deliverable: The working pipeline, the generated HTML report, and the technical documentation.
Reflection: What was the ratio of AI generation time to your debugging time? What are the risks of deploying AI-generated pipelines without thorough review?




Exercise 7 — Custom MCP Server Design

Day 3 · Expert · ~60 min · Tools: A text editor

Goal: Design (on paper) an MCP server that safely connects an AI agent to a public-health data system.
Why it matters: MCP lets AI assistants reach external data through a standard interface. Designing one forces careful thinking about access, security, and appropriate automation.
Steps

Choose a scenario: Disease Surveillance Assistant, Community Health Assessment Tool, or Environmental Health Inspector Support.
Specify 6–10 tools. For each, define name, description, parameters (with types), return structure, data source, and access control. For example:

get_case_counts(disease: str, start_date: date, end_date: date, geography: str) → {counts by area} — reads the reportable-disease database; read-only; aggregate counts only, never individual records.

Define security constraints: what the agent must never access, what requires human confirmation, how it handles requests for protected health information (PHI), and what audit logging is required.
Write the agent’s system prompt (500–800 words): role and expertise, rules for tool use, data-interpretation guidance, explicit restrictions, and edge-case handling.
Compare capabilities: a table of tasks the agent could do vs. how they are done today, with rough time savings.

Hints

Default every tool to read-only and aggregate; make any write or record-level access require explicit human confirmation.
Write the restrictions (“never do X”) as concretely as the capabilities — vague limits are where agents get into trouble.

Done when

6–10 tools fully specified
Security constraints and audit logging defined
System prompt and capability comparison written

Deliverable: A 3–5 page design document: use case, MCP spec, security constraints, system prompt, capability comparison.
Reflection: What governance would need to exist before deploying an agent with access to real public-health data systems?




Exercise 8 — Local vs. Cloud Model Evaluation

Day 3 · Intermediate · ~45 min · Tools: Ollama (gemma4:12b or qwen3:8b), Claude or ChatGPT

Goal: Measure the real quality gap between a local model and a cloud model, so you know when privacy is worth the tradeoff.
Why it matters: Knowing where local models are “good enough” lets you decide when data privacy justifies reduced capability.
Steps

Write 10 test prompts — two each in: factual knowledge, data interpretation (give a small table), writing/summarization, multi-step reasoning, and code generation (working R or Python).
Run all 10 on both a local model in Ollama and your cloud model, using identical text. Score each 1–5 on accuracy, completeness, relevance, and safety. Record response times.
Summarize and recommend. Build a table, compute the average score gap by category, and write a 300–400 word memo: where local is adequate, where cloud is clearly better, and when staff should default to each.

Hints

Pull the local model ahead of time (ollama pull gemma4:12b); first load is slow.
The gap is usually smallest on summarization and largest on multi-step reasoning and code — check whether your results agree.

Done when

10 prompts run on both models
Scoring table with average gap by category
Recommendation memo written

Deliverable: The 10 prompts, scoring table, summary analysis, and recommendation memo.
Reflection: How might results change with a larger local model (e.g., a 30B+ on a high-RAM machine)? Are there tasks where local is preferable beyond privacy?




Exercise 9 — AI-Assisted Grant Proposal Section

Day 3 · Advanced · ~75 min · Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, Perplexity

Goal: Draft a Specific Aims page and Research Strategy section using AI iteratively — as a drafting partner, not a one-shot generator.
Why it matters: Grant writing is time-intensive. AI helps a lot, but only when used iteratively with your expertise in the loop.
Steps

Pick a topic at the intersection of AI and urban health relevant to Philadelphia. Use Perplexity to find 5–8 key references and state the literature gap.
Draft Specific Aims iteratively:

Draft a 1-page NIH Specific Aims for a study on {topic}: an opening paragraph establishing the problem, an overarching goal, and three aims, each with a short rationale and a hypothesis.

Then revise at least three elements with your own field knowledge, and track your edits.
Draft a 2-page Significance/Innovation the same way. For each paragraph, label it: AI-generated with minor edits, substantially rewritten, or human-written.

Hints

Feed the AI your references and field knowledge first — ungrounded aims read generic and cite phantom literature.
Keep a prompt log; you will reuse the good prompts on your next proposal.

Done when

1-page Specific Aims drafted and edited
2-page Significance/Innovation with per-paragraph authorship labels
Prompt log saved

Deliverable: Specific Aims, Significance/Innovation, a prompt log, and a short reflection.
Reflection: Where was AI most and least helpful? How would you disclose AI use in a grant submission (check current NIH policy)?




Exercise 13 — Create a Skill + a Scheduled Task

Day 3 · Intermediate · ~45 min · Tools: Claude (claude.ai / Claude Desktop / Cowork) or Claude Code

Goal: Package one of your recurring tasks as a reusable Skill, then (optionally) wire it into a scheduled task that runs on its own.
Why it matters: A Skill captures your way of doing a task so the agent repeats it consistently across conversations and tools — and a scheduled task turns a one-off into standing automation. Together they move you from “ask each time” to “set it up once.”
Steps

Pick a recurring task with a consistent structure — e.g., “format a weekly surveillance summary,” “de-identify a dataset to our standard,” or “turn meeting notes into an action list.”
Author a SKILL.md. Create a folder with a SKILL.md that has YAML frontmatter and instructions:
---
name: weekly-surveillance-summary
description: Format raw weekly case counts into our standard departmental summary. Use when the user provides weekly surveillance numbers.
---
# Weekly Surveillance Summary
Steps:
1. Produce a one-paragraph plain-language summary.
2. Add a table: indicator, this week, last week, % change.
3. Flag any indicator up >20% week-over-week.
Tone: factual, no alarm. Never invent numbers not provided.
Add optional references/ (a template) or scripts/ (a helper script) if useful. Shortcut: use the Skill Creator (built into Claude Desktop/Cowork; /plugin install skill-creator in Claude Code) to generate and refine it for you.
Install and test it. Upload the zipped folder on claude.ai (Settings → Customize → Skills), or point Claude Code/Cowork at it. Start a fresh conversation, give it a realistic input, and confirm the skill triggers and produces your format. If it doesn’t trigger, sharpen the description (it controls when the skill fires) and retest.
(Optional) Schedule it. In Cowork (desktop, paid plan), turn the task into a recurring task: describe it once, pick a cadence (e.g., every Monday), and confirm each run can reach your files/skills. Schedule only work that’s safe to run with a human reviewing the output afterward.

Hints

The description is the most important line — it decides when the skill activates. Be specific about the trigger.
De-identify any real data before it goes into the model, and bake that into the skill’s instructions.
Keep SKILL.md in version control so you can track changes over time.

Done when

A SKILL.md (plus any resources) that triggers reliably on a fresh, realistic input
A before/after example showing the skill’s output
(Optional) A scheduled/recurring task configured, or a short note on what you’d schedule and how often

Deliverable: Your skill folder, one worked example, and a note on which tasks are good vs. poor candidates for a skill or a schedule.






NoteFree-tier note



Authoring a SKILL.md is free for anyone (it’s just a folder). Using skills in claude.ai and Cowork scheduled tasks generally require a paid plan and the desktop app — if you don’t have access, the instructor will demo this part.


Reflection: Which of your tasks are good candidates for a skill, and which should never be automated on a schedule without review? What could go wrong if a scheduled task runs on stale or bad input?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 4 Challenges


Exercise 10 — Build an Interactive Public Health Dashboard

Day 4 · Advanced · ~90 min · Tools: Claude Code or ChatGPT, HVI data, a web browser

Goal: Turn heat-vulnerability data into a self-contained interactive dashboard — and design the story it tells.
Why it matters: Dashboards let stakeholders explore data themselves. AI can write the code; your job is the design and the narrative.
Steps

Plan. Decide the audience, the 3–5 questions the dashboard answers, and which variables to show. Sketch a wireframe and write a 150–200 word design brief.
Build. Use the Philadelphia HVI CSV. Share your brief and data and ask:

Build a single self-contained HTML file (inline CSS/JS, no external dependencies except a CDN charting library) from this CSV: 3–4 KPI cards, a histogram of HVI scores, a scatterplot of two components, one more visualization (map or comparison), at least one dropdown filter, and a short narrative. Make it colorblind-friendly and clearly labeled.

Iterate to fix issues and improve styling.
Review. Can a non-technical viewer get the takeaway in 30 seconds? Are charts labeled, colors accessible, and the narrative contextual?

Hints

Ask for one self-contained .html file so it opens with a double-click and is easy to share.
Give the AI a few real rows of the CSV so it builds the parser around the actual columns.

Done when

Dashboard opens in a browser and is interactive
Passes the 30-second-takeaway and accessibility checks
Design brief and wireframe saved

Deliverable: The self-contained HTML dashboard, plus your design brief and wireframe.
Reflection: How would you adapt this for a community vs. a technical audience? What data-quality caveats should travel with the dashboard?




Exercise 12 — Personal AI Workflow Automation

Day 4 · Intermediate · ~60 min · Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, tools from your daily work

Goal: Turn one recurring task from your real job into a reusable, quality-checked AI workflow.
Why it matters: The highest-impact AI integration targets specific, repeated, structured tasks — not vague “use AI more.”
Steps

Pick a task. List 3–5 recurring tasks with frequency, duration, input type, and whether output is internal or public. Choose the best candidate (high time, high repetition, low risk).
Build a workflow package:

a reusable prompt template (PTCF structure) with placeholders for variable inputs;
a pre-processing checklist (de-identify, format, gather context);
a quality checklist of 5–8 checks (accuracy, completeness, tone, safety);
one fully worked example (filled prompt → AI output → final after your review).

Test and validate on three real, de-identified examples. Record time with vs. without the workflow, quality, and any failures. Estimate annual time saved.

Hints

De-identify inputs before they ever reach the model — bake that into the pre-processing checklist.
A workflow a colleague can run from your template is far more valuable than a one-off prompt.

Done when

Prompt template + both checklists written
Three tested examples with timing data
Annual time-savings estimate

Deliverable: Prompt template, checklists, three tested examples with timing, and the savings estimate.
Reflection: What training would a new colleague need to use your workflow? How would you monitor its quality over time?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 5 Challenges


Exercise 11 — Adversarial AI Testing for Public Health Safety

Day 5 · Advanced · ~60 min · Tools: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini

Goal: Systematically probe AI for dangerous, biased, or misleading outputs before anyone would rely on it.
Why it matters: Before deploying AI in public-health settings you need to know how it fails. Adversarial testing finds the failures on purpose, in a safe setting.
Steps

Design a test protocol — two prompts in each of five failure categories (10 total):

Inappropriate clinical advice (specific recommendations the AI shouldn’t give), Biased recommendations (does advice change by race, neighborhood, or income?), Statistical hallucination (asks for local stats that may not exist), Privacy violations (tries to get identifying information), Overconfidence (does it communicate uncertainty?).

Execute each prompt on at least two platforms. For each, record the exact prompt, the full response, pass/fail, severity (low/medium/high/critical), and the guardrail that would prevent the failure.
Write a 1-page safety assessment: executive summary; a risk matrix (categories × platforms, with severity); 3–5 key findings with examples; mitigation recommendations; and a deployment recommendation (deploy / deploy with conditions / do not deploy).

Hints

Keep these tests in a sandbox — the point is to surface failures, not to act on any output.
Severity matters more than pass-rate: one critical failure outweighs many minor ones.

Done when

10 prompts across five categories, run on 2+ platforms
Results documented with severity ratings
1-page safety assessment with a deployment call

Deliverable: The test protocol, documented results, and the 1-page safety assessment.
Reflection: How comprehensive was your testing? What might you have missed? Should safety testing be done by the team that will use the AI, or by an independent group?
This exercise pairs with the “three inverse laws” from Day 1 — especially non-deference (verify before trusting) and non-abdication (you stay responsible).

Image analysis, data analysis with AI, qualitative methods, and document analysis
