title: Day 2: Multimodal AI & Working with Models
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of today, you will be able to:

Use AI tools for image annotation, generation, and analysis in public health contexts
Conduct data analysis using AI-assisted tools with real Philadelphia health data
Generate code (R, Python, or Stata) using AI to perform statistical analyses
Apply AI to qualitative data analysis tasks (thematic coding, memo generation)
Use AI to analyze and synthesize long policy documents at scale
Evaluate the accuracy and limitations of AI-generated analyses

title: Day 2: Multimodal AI & Working with Models
text:
Lecture: Multimodal AI Capabilities

Beyond Text: AI That Sees, Hears, and Analyzes
Modern AI systems are increasingly multimodal—they can process and generate not just text, but images, audio, video, and structured data. For public health, this opens up capabilities including:

Image analysis: Classifying housing conditions from street-level imagery, identifying environmental hazards, analyzing medical images
Computer vision for urban health: Satellite imagery for environmental monitoring, pedestrian safety audits from video, identifying food desert locations
Audio/video: Transcription of community meetings, analysis of public health messaging
Tabular data analysis: Exploratory data analysis, statistical modeling, visualization—all through natural language
Deep Research tools: AI-powered research assistants that can search, synthesize, and cite (e.g., Perplexity, ChatGPT Deep Research, Gemini Deep Research)







TipWorking with Models: Build On, Don’t Build



You almost never need to train your own model. The practical skill is adapting and connecting existing foundation models to your data and tools.
Four ways to put a model to work (climb only as far as you need):

Prompt / context engineering — instructions, examples, and system prompts. Start here.
Retrieval (RAG) — let the model read your documents at query time (e.g., NotebookLM).
Tools — give it actions: web search, code execution, database queries (the focus of Day 3).
Fine-tuning — further-train a smaller model for a narrow, repeated task.

Open & local models. For sensitive health data, a capable open model run locally (via Ollama or LM Studio — e.g., Gemma 4, Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek) keeps data on your machine, often the responsible default. See Resources for setup.
When to use what: one-off drafting → a frontier chat model; answers grounded in your documents → retrieval/projects; large-scale processing → API + scripting; sensitive data → a local model; multi-step work across tools → an agent (Day 3).


Today’s Approach
We will work with real Philadelphia data and realistic public health scenarios. The goal is not to replace your statistical training, but to show how AI can accelerate your workflow—and where you still need human judgment.

title: Day 2: Multimodal AI & Working with Models
text:
Today’s Approach
We will work with real Philadelphia data and realistic public health scenarios. The goal is not to replace your statistical training, but to show how AI can accelerate your workflow—and where you still need human judgment.

title: Day 2: Multimodal AI & Working with Models
text:
Activity 1: Image Annotation

Activity 1: Image Annotation

Duration: ~30 minutes | Format: Hands-on comparison exercise

Goal: Compare manual and AI-assisted image annotation approaches relevant to urban health research.
Sample image: We will use an aerial view of a busy urban intersection. Download the image.



Aerial view of an urban intersection with crosswalks, bus lanes, vehicles, motorcycles, and pedestrians — the sample image for this activity.


An example annotation export (COCO-format JSON from MakeSense.ai, with labels for crosswalks, traffic lanes, motor vehicles, pedestrians, and bus stops) is available for instructors as a reference.

Part A: Manual Annotation with MakeSense.ai (~15 min)

Go to MakeSense.ai — a free, browser-based image annotation tool
Upload the sample image above (or your own urban streetscape, housing-condition, or environmental-feature image)
Practice creating:

Bounding boxes around objects of interest (e.g., crosswalks, traffic lanes, motor vehicles, pedestrians, bus stops)
Labels for each annotation

Export your annotations (try COCO JSON, the format of the example export above)



Part B: AI-Assisted Annotation with Google AI Studio (~15 min)

Go to Google AI Studio
Upload the same image
Use Gemini’s Spatial Understanding capabilities. Try prompts like:


Analyze this aerial image of an urban intersection and identify all features relevant to pedestrian safety. For each feature, provide its approximate location in the image and classify it as either a “safety asset” (e.g., crosswalk, bus lane, traffic signal) or a “safety concern” (e.g., a long unmarked crossing, vehicles encroaching on the crosswalk, missing curb cut).


Compare the AI-generated annotations with your manual ones



Discussion

How accurate were the AI annotations compared to your manual ones?
What did the AI miss? What did it incorrectly identify?
In what scenarios might each approach be more appropriate?
What are the implications of annotation errors in public health research?

title: Day 2: Multimodal AI & Working with Models
text:
Activity 2: Image Generation

Activity 2: Image Generation

Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Individual exploration with group reflection

Goal: Explore AI image generation for public health communications and understand its ethical dimensions.

Instructions

Use an image-generation-capable platform (ChatGPT image generation, Gemini, or another tool) to create images for a public health campaign. Try prompts like:


Create a warm, inviting illustration for a flyer promoting free blood pressure screenings at a community health fair in a diverse urban neighborhood. The image should show people of different ages and backgrounds in a welcoming community setting. Do not include any text in the image.


Iterate and refine: Modify your prompt to improve the result. Try specifying:

Art style (illustration vs. photograph-like)
Specific demographic representation
Setting details
What to exclude

Critical evaluation: For each generated image, ask:

Does it accurately represent the community it is intended for?
Are there stereotypes or biases in the representation?
Would you feel comfortable using this in an official health department communication?
What are the ethical considerations of using AI-generated images in public health materials?








NoteFree-tier note



Gemini and Google AI Studio offer free image generation. ChatGPT’s free tier includes limited image generation; if you hit a limit, Microsoft Copilot (free) also generates images, or your instructor will demonstrate.








WarningEthical Considerations



AI-generated images can perpetuate stereotypes and may not accurately represent specific communities. Always review generated images critically, seek community input when possible, and consider whether stock photos or real community photos might be more appropriate. Disclose AI-generated imagery when used in official materials.

title: Day 2: Multimodal AI & Working with Models
text:
Activity 3: Data Analysis with AI

Activity 3: Data Analysis with AI

Duration: ~50 minutes | Format: Guided hands-on analysis







NoteFree-tier note



Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini free tiers all accept CSV/Excel uploads for this activity (subject to file-size and daily message limits). Google AI Studio (free) also works well. No paid plan is required.


Goal: Use AI to analyze a real Philadelphia dataset through progressive prompting, from exploration to code generation.

The Dataset: Philadelphia Heat Vulnerability Index
We will work with the real Heat Vulnerability Index (HVI) from OpenDataPhilly — 384 Philadelphia census tracts (2023).
Get the data: philadelphia_hvi.csv (a cleaned table, ready to upload), or the original Heat Vulnerability by Census Tract on OpenDataPhilly. For mapping, also grab the GeoJSON.
Each row is one tract: geoid10 (tract ID), name10 (tract number), hsi_score (heat sensitivity index), hei_score (heat exposure index), hvi_score (overall vulnerability), n_veryhigh (count of very-high-vulnerability blocks), and year. The overall HVI combines the exposure and sensitivity indices.






Note



This published file contains the composite indices only — not the underlying drivers (poverty, tree canopy, surface temperature). To analyze those drivers you would join other datasets, which is exactly Bonus Exercise 3.




Step-by-Step Progressive Prompting
Upload philadelphia_hvi.csv to your preferred AI platform (Claude and ChatGPT both handle CSV uploads well) and work through these prompts in order:
Step 1: Explore

I have uploaded the Philadelphia Heat Vulnerability Index dataset. Examine it and tell me: how many records are there, what variables are included, what is the unit of analysis, and are there any missing values or data-quality issues?

Step 2: Describe

Provide descriptive statistics for the numeric variables. Which census tracts have the highest and lowest overall HVI scores (give the geoid10 and name10)? Describe the distribution of hvi_score.

Step 3: Analyze

The overall HVI combines a heat-exposure index (hei_score) and a heat-sensitivity index (hsi_score). Examine the relationship between exposure and sensitivity: are they correlated? Which tracts score high on BOTH? How does each relate to the overall hvi_score?

Step 4: Visualize

Create: (1) a histogram of hvi_score; (2) a scatter plot of hei_score (x-axis) vs hsi_score (y-axis), with points sized or colored by hvi_score; (3) a horizontal bar chart of the 15 tracts with the highest hvi_score, labeled by name10.

Step 5: Generate Code + Map

Write code in [R / Python / Stata] that: (1) loads philadelphia_hvi.csv; (2) computes summary statistics by quintile of hvi_score; (3) makes the top-15-tracts bar chart; and (4) joins the table to philadelphia_hvi.geojson on geoid10 and draws a choropleth map of hvi_score across Philadelphia.


R UsersPython UsersStata Users


Ask the AI to generate code using tidyverse, ggplot2, and sf (for spatial data).


Ask for code using pandas, matplotlib/seaborn, and geopandas.


Ask for Stata .do file syntax with appropriate labels and output formatting.





Verification Checklist
After each step, check:

Do the numbers make sense given what you know about Philadelphia?
Are the variable interpretations correct?
Is the statistical approach appropriate?
Would you trust this analysis enough to present it to your supervisor?







ImportantAlways Verify AI-Generated Analysis



AI can produce plausible-looking but incorrect analyses. Common issues include: misinterpreting variable names, applying inappropriate statistical tests, generating code with subtle errors, and presenting fabricated statistics. Always verify key findings independently.

Activity 4: Qualitative Data Analysis (Optional Track)

Activity 4: Qualitative Data Analysis (Optional Track)

Duration: ~40 minutes | Format: Hands-on practice with critical discussion

Goal: Explore how AI can assist with qualitative data analysis while understanding its significant limitations.






Note



This activity is an optional deeper dive for participants interested in qualitative research methods. Others may continue with Activity 5.



Instructions

Upload a transcript to Claude or ChatGPT. You may use:

The provided sample: a fictional focus group on the health impacts of climate change — download (.txt) or (.md). It covers health impacts residents experience, how they want to respond, and reactions to four different message framings for adaptation and mitigation.
Your own de-identified qualitative data

Initial thematic coding: Ask the AI to identify preliminary themes:


I am going to share a transcript from a community focus group about the health impacts of climate change. Please read through it and identify the major themes that emerge. For each theme, provide: (1) a theme name, (2) a brief description, (3) two to three supporting quotes from the text.


Message testing: This transcript also tested four different message framings. Ask the AI to analyze them:


The facilitator tested four message framings (A–D) for communicating about climate and health. Summarize how participants reacted to each, note where reactions differed by age or lived experience, and recommend which framing to use for which audience.


Code refinement: After reviewing the initial themes, ask the AI to:


Review the themes you identified. Are there any that overlap and should be combined? Are there any that are too broad and should be split? Reorganize the coding framework and provide a revised codebook with definitions for each code.


Compare with manual coding: If you have experience with qualitative coding, compare:

What themes did the AI identify that you would have missed (or vice versa)?
How do the AI-generated code definitions compare to what you would write?
Where does the AI’s interpretation seem superficial or inaccurate?

Memo generation: Ask the AI to draft an analytic memo:


Based on the themes identified, draft an analytic memo (300 words) discussing the relationships between themes and how they connect to existing literature on climate change, health equity, and risk communication in under-resourced urban communities.



Critical Discussion






WarningLimitations of AI for Qualitative Analysis



Jowsey et al. (2024) found that while AI can assist with initial coding, it often lacks the contextual understanding, cultural sensitivity, and interpretive depth that experienced qualitative researchers bring. AI-generated codes tend to be more descriptive than interpretive and may miss nuance, power dynamics, and embodied meaning.
AI should be viewed as a starting point or sounding board, not a replacement for rigorous qualitative methodology.



How comfortable are you with AI-generated qualitative analysis?
In what ways could AI assist (not replace) a qualitative researcher?
What are the ethical implications of using AI to analyze participants’ words?

title: Day 4: AI for Urban Health Workflows
text:
Lecture: Co-Intelligence and Workflow Design

Mollick’s Four Rules of Co-Intelligence
Ethan Mollick (Wharton, University of Pennsylvania) proposes four principles for working with AI effectively. These form a practical framework for deciding how to integrate AI into your work:
Rule 1: Always invite AI to the table. Try AI for any task—you will be surprised how often it helps. The cost of trying is low; the cost of missing a useful application is high.
Rule 2: Be the human in the loop. AI makes mistakes, hallucinates, and lacks judgment. You provide the expertise, context, and ethical reasoning. Use AI as a first draft, a thought partner, or an accelerator—never as the final word.
Rule 3: Treat AI like a person (but a person who is sometimes confidently wrong). AI works best when you interact with it conversationally: provide context, ask follow-up questions, give feedback, and iterate. But never forget it can produce plausible-sounding nonsense.
Rule 4: Assume this is the worst AI you will ever use. Today’s tools are the floor, not the ceiling. Build workflows that take advantage of current capabilities while remaining adaptable as tools improve rapidly.


What to Automate vs. What to Keep Human
Not every task benefits from AI. A useful framework:







Automate with AI
Keep Human




First drafts of reports and memos
Final review and approval


Literature search and synthesis
Critical interpretation


Data cleaning and exploratory analysis
Study design and causal inference


Scheduling and routine communications
Sensitive stakeholder conversations


Translation and plain-language summaries
Community engagement strategy


Template generation
Policy decisions


Formatting and copy editing
Ethical judgment calls









ImportantThe Accountability Principle



If something goes wrong with an AI-assisted output—a factual error in a report, a biased recommendation, an inappropriate communication—the human who approved and published it is responsible. AI does not bear accountability. This is why human oversight is non-negotiable.

title: Day 4: AI for Urban Health Workflows
text:
Activity 2: Scenario-Based Small Group Work


Activity 2: Scenario-Based Small Group Work

Duration: ~75 minutes | Format: Groups of 3–5 people

Goal: Develop a comprehensive AI-assisted approach to a realistic urban health challenge.

Instructions
Each group will be assigned one of the five scenarios below (or may choose the qualitative option). Your group should:

Read your scenario carefully (5 min)
Discuss the challenges and brainstorm AI applications (15 min)
Build and test at least 2–3 AI-assisted solutions using the tools you have learned (35 min)
Prepare a 3-minute presentation for the class (10 min)
Present (see Activity 3)

For your presentation, address:

What AI tools did you use and why?
Show one concrete example of something you built/generated
What worked well? What did not?
What human oversight is essential?
What ethical concerns arose?





Scenario 1: Childhood Vaccination Outreach

Scenario 1: Childhood Vaccination Outreach
Setting: You are a team at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health working on childhood immunization. Philadelphia’s childhood vaccination rates for the DTaP and MMR series have fallen below the Healthy People 2030 targets in several ZIP codes, particularly in North and West Philadelphia.
Key Challenges:

Identifying under-vaccinated communities using available data
Combating vaccine misinformation circulating on social media
Creating culturally appropriate outreach materials in English, Spanish, and Haitian Creole
Coordinating with schools, clinics, and community organizations
Tracking outreach effectiveness

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze vaccination coverage data by ZIP code to prioritize outreach areas
Generate multilingual outreach materials (flyers, social media, text messages)
Create a FAQ document addressing common vaccine concerns with evidence-based responses
Draft talking points for community health workers
Design a data dashboard to track outreach metrics

Guiding Questions:

How would you verify the accuracy of AI-generated health information in multiple languages?
What community voices need to be centered in this work that AI cannot replace?
How do you balance urgency with cultural sensitivity?

Suggested Tools: ChatGPT, Claude, Google AI Studio, Perplexity for research




Scenario 2: Type 2 Diabetes Prevention

Scenario 2: Type 2 Diabetes Prevention
Setting: You are developing a neighborhood-level diabetes prevention strategy for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Type 2 diabetes rates are significantly higher in low-income neighborhoods with limited access to healthy food and safe spaces for physical activity.
Key Challenges:

Identifying neighborhoods with the highest diabetes burden and fewest prevention resources
Understanding the intersection of food access, built environment, and health outcomes
Developing targeted interventions that account for community-specific barriers
Creating educational materials that resonate with diverse populations
Evaluating program effectiveness

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze census tract-level diabetes prevalence alongside food access and built environment data
Generate community-specific intervention recommendations based on evidence reviews
Create a risk assessment tool that community health workers can use during home visits
Draft a grant proposal for a community-based diabetes prevention program
Develop culturally tailored nutrition education content

Guiding Questions:

How would you ensure AI-generated recommendations account for structural determinants of health?
What data would you need to make this work, and what are the privacy considerations?
How do you involve community members in validating AI-suggested interventions?

Suggested Tools: Claude (data analysis), ChatGPT (content generation), Perplexity (evidence review)




Scenario 3: Heat Vulnerability During Heatwaves

Scenario 3: Heat Vulnerability During Heatwaves
Setting: You are part of Philadelphia’s climate and health team preparing for the summer heat season. Philadelphia has experienced increasingly severe heat waves, and heat-related illness and death disproportionately affect low-income communities, elderly residents, and people with chronic conditions.
Key Challenges:

Using the Heat Vulnerability Index to target cooling interventions
Communicating heat risk to vulnerable populations
Coordinating cooling center operations across the city
Monitoring heat-related emergency department visits in real time
Evaluating the effectiveness of heat response strategies

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze the Philadelphia HVI data to identify priority neighborhoods
Generate targeted messaging for different vulnerable populations (elderly, outdoor workers, people experiencing homelessness)
Create a decision support tool for when to activate heat emergency protocols
Draft a heat action plan with specific triggers and responses
Build a monitoring dashboard that integrates weather forecasts with health data

Guiding Questions:

How accurate does AI-generated risk communication need to be when lives are at stake?
What real-time data feeds would make an AI-powered heat response system most effective?
How do you reach people who are most vulnerable but least connected to digital communications?

Suggested Tools: Claude (data analysis of HVI dataset), ChatGPT (communications), Google AI Studio (agent building)




Scenario 4: Pedestrian Injury Prevention

Scenario 4: Pedestrian Injury Prevention
Setting: You work on injury prevention at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Pedestrian fatalities have been increasing, with disproportionate impact on older adults and communities of color. The city is developing a Vision Zero strategy.
Key Challenges:

Identifying high-risk corridors and intersections using crash data
Understanding the built environment factors that contribute to pedestrian injuries
Engaging communities in identifying dangerous locations (crowdsourced data)
Developing evidence-based infrastructure and policy recommendations
Creating public awareness campaigns

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze crash data to identify spatial and temporal patterns
Use image analysis to assess street-level safety features (crosswalks, lighting, traffic signals)
Generate community survey instruments for pedestrian safety assessments
Draft policy briefs recommending specific infrastructure improvements
Create multilingual safety campaign materials targeting high-risk areas

Guiding Questions:

How could AI analysis of crash data complement traditional epidemiological approaches?
What are the risks of using AI to prioritize infrastructure investments (potential for bias)?
How do you incorporate community knowledge that may not be captured in official data?

Suggested Tools: Claude (data analysis), Google AI Studio (image analysis), ChatGPT (content generation)




Scenario 5: Foodborne Illness Detection

Scenario 5: Foodborne Illness Detection
Setting: You are on the foodborne illness investigation team at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. The team investigates reports of food poisoning, inspects food establishments, and monitors for outbreaks. The current process involves manual review of complaint reports, which is time-consuming and sometimes delays response.
Key Challenges:

Processing and triaging large volumes of consumer complaints quickly
Identifying potential outbreak clusters from individual reports
Prioritizing restaurant inspections based on risk indicators
Communicating with the public about food safety during outbreaks
Documenting investigations for regulatory purposes

Suggested AI Applications:

Build a complaint triage system that categorizes and prioritizes reports
Analyze patterns in complaint data to detect potential clusters
Generate investigation checklists based on reported symptoms and food types
Draft public health advisories when outbreaks are confirmed
Create standardized investigation reports from field notes

Guiding Questions:

What level of AI autonomy is appropriate for public health surveillance?
How do you balance speed (AI triage) with accuracy (human review)?
What happens if the AI system misses a cluster or generates a false alarm?

Suggested Tools: Claude (text analysis of complaints), ChatGPT (document generation), Google AI Studio (classification agent)




Qualitative Option: Community Health Assessment Interviews

Qualitative Option: Analyzing Community Health Assessment Interview Data
For groups with qualitative research interest. Replace one of the above scenarios with this option.
Setting: Your team has conducted 30 semi-structured interviews with residents of a Philadelphia neighborhood as part of a Community Health Needs Assessment. You need to analyze the data and produce a report for community stakeholders and the health department.
Key Challenges:

Coding 30 interviews efficiently with limited qualitative research staff
Ensuring rigor and trustworthiness of the analysis
Identifying themes that reflect community priorities (not just researcher assumptions)
Translating findings into actionable recommendations
Presenting findings back to the community in accessible formats

Suggested AI Applications:

Use AI for initial deductive coding based on interview guides
Generate preliminary theme summaries for researcher review
Identify illustrative quotes for each theme
Draft the findings section of the community report
Create plain-language summary documents for community partners

Guiding Questions:

What role should AI play in interpreting community members’ experiences?
How do you maintain the rigor expected of qualitative research when using AI tools?
What would you need to disclose in a methods section about AI-assisted analysis?

Try It: Research-Workflow Demos
Three concrete ways to put agentic AI to work in your own research. Try the chat-based versions on a free tier; the file-building demo will be shown live by the instructor.

Teach it your voice. Give the model a few of your own writing samples and have it draft in your style — an abstract, an email, a grant aim. You edit; it learns the pattern.
CV → website. Hand an agent your CV and ask it to build a simple academic site (structure, pages, styling), then refine it by conversation.
Live research brainstorm. Talk through a study idea with the model as a thinking partner: rival hypotheses, confounders, designs, and what would falsify it.

Adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).

WarningNIH Policy on AI in Grant Writing



NIH and other funders are developing policies on AI use in grant applications. As of 2025, NIH requires disclosure of AI tool use in the preparation of grant applications. Always check the current funder requirements before submitting. AI cannot serve as a reviewer or be listed as a co-investigator.

title: AI for Urban Health
text:
Welcome
Welcome to AI for Urban Health, a week-long intensive course offered through Drexel University’s Urban Health Collaborative (UHC) Summer Institute. This course is designed for public health professionals—particularly those working in urban settings—who want to understand, evaluate, and practically apply artificial intelligence tools in their daily work.
Over five days, you will move from foundational AI concepts to hands-on experience with cutting-edge tools, culminating in practical workflow integration and responsible use frameworks. No prior programming experience is required.
New here? Start with the orientation: 📊 Welcome & Orientation slides ↗ — how the course works, what software you’ll need, and how to get set up.

Pre-Course Checklist
Please complete the following before Day 1:

WarningImportant: Data Privacy



Throughout this course, we will use AI platforms that send data to external servers. Never enter protected health information (PHI), personally identifiable information (PII), or other sensitive data into any AI tool unless you are using a locally hosted model (covered in Day 3) or a platform with a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA).
When working with real-world examples, always de-identify data first. We will discuss data privacy in detail throughout the course and dedicate significant time to it on Day 5.

All CLI tools require Node.js (version 18 or later). Install via:
# Install Node.js (if not already installed)

Ollama lets you run large language models locally on your computer. Your data never leaves your machine (no data is sent to any external server), making it suitable for sensitive data—though the models are generally less capable than cloud-based alternatives.






Platform
Method




macOS
brew install ollama or download from ollama.com


Windows
Download installer


Linux
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh \| sh







Minimum: 8 GB RAM, 5 GB free disk space
Recommended: 16 GB RAM, 10+ GB free disk space
For larger models: 32 GB RAM or Apple Silicon Mac with unified memory




# Start Ollama (runs in background)
ollama serve

# Download and run a model (pick one that fits your laptop — see table below)
ollama run qwen3           # Alibaba Qwen3 — strong, good at structured output/tools
ollama run gemma4          # Google Gemma 4 — efficient, laptop-friendly
ollama run qwen3:4b        # smaller size for 8 GB laptops
ollama run llama3.1:8b     # Meta Llama, general purpose

# List downloaded models; see if one is running on GPU or CPU
ollama list
ollama ps

# Remove a model
ollama rm model-name






Note



Open models change fast. The names above are current as of mid-2026 — always check ollama.com/library for the latest tags and sizes. The strongest open-weight model right now, GLM-5.2 (MIT license, ~744B mixture-of-experts), is a datacenter/cloud-class model: run it via Ollama Cloud or a hosted API, not on a laptop.





Rough guide using 4-bit quantization (Q4_K_M, Ollama’s default) — a model needs roughly 0.6 GB of memory per billion parameters, plus headroom for context.








Your laptop
Usable memory
Models that run well (Q4)




Entry (8 GB RAM, no/integrated GPU)
~5 GB
Gemma 4 small, Qwen3 4B, Phi-4-mini, Llama 3.2 3B


Mainstream (16 GB RAM, or 8 GB GPU)
~10 GB

Strong (32 GB RAM, or 12–16 GB GPU)
~20 GB

Workstation (64 GB+ unified, or 24 GB GPU)
40 GB+
70B-class or large MoE, heavily quantized


Not a laptop (cloud/server)
—
GLM-5.2, DeepSeek V4, MiniMax M3, Llama 4 Maverick



On a Mac (Apple Silicon — M1/M2/M3/M4): memory is unified (shared by CPU and GPU) and Ollama uses the GPU (Metal) automatically. Plan to use about 60–70% of total RAM for the model — a 16 GB Mac handles up to ~8–14B comfortably, a 32 GB Mac up to ~32B. Intel Macs are CPU-only; stick to 3–4B models.
On a PC (Windows / Linux): with an NVIDIA GPU, the limit is VRAM — the model should mostly fit in VRAM for good speed (check with nvidia-smi). Roughly: 8 GB VRAM → up to 8B, 12 GB → up to 14B, 24 GB (e.g., RTX 4090) → up to ~32B. Without a dedicated GPU, Ollama runs on CPU + system RAM — fine for 3–8B models, just slower. AMD GPUs work via ROCm on Linux and recent Windows builds.



Once Ollama is running, this Jupyter notebook builds real agentic workflows entirely on a local model — structured-output triage, a multi-agent crew (CrewAI), a state machine (LangGraph), and a tool-calling agent — on an everyday triage task you choose: 311 complaints, food-safety violations, or pedestrian-injury surveillance. Everything runs on your machine, free and private. Open it in Positron, VS Code, or Jupyter. It is also linked from Day 3 as an optional advanced track.



If you cannot install software on your work computer:

Google AI Studio: Free, browser-based, runs Gemini models. Reasonable privacy policies for non-sensitive work.
HuggingChat: Free, runs open-source models in the browser.
Poe: Access to multiple models through a single interface.







Note



Browser-based alternatives still send data to external servers. They are more private than consumer chatbots in some cases, but they are not equivalent to running models locally. For truly sensitive data, use Ollama or discuss options with your IT department.
